This game has definitely become one of my favorite metroidvanias. I've noticed a lot of people complaining about the precision needed and big difficulty spikes, but in my opinion its what makes this game so good. Everything is doable its just going to take you time. The combat and platforming both feel incredibly solid, they do require a good bit of time to fully adjust to, but the fulfillment you get after completing a crazy platforming section or a crazy boss is, in my opinion, second to none. I do wish this game got more love as I think its rather expertly crafted. I have some minor qualms about some mechanics, [spoiler] (the gravity stuff in the planets was cool but good lord that was disorienting and could've been accomplished a bit better) [/spoiler] but the fact that every part of the game felt fresh and challenging throughout made this an amazing experience.  To be frank though, if you like metroidvanias, or souls like metroidvanias, but do not like precision platformers, or vise versa, you probably won't like this game. This is a game where you are regaining your godhood and you can feel it in the abilities and progression, but you are also challenging the very nature of the world and its other gods. Which remains a rightfully difficult feat.  As for what I believe to be more minor considerations in my enjoyment of the game but still relevant, the music score, art style, and story are all very enjoyable. The world feels very realized, albeit with some pretty large amounts of high fantasy. But most notably for me is that each area felt fresh and new rather than a rif on the last area. The scenery can change greatly and I like that. The score is well done. Although there are times where the tracks begin to grate on you in the more difficult platforming sections, each track suits its area well. As for the story, I personally got pretty invested with the twists and turns this pseudo yin and yang endless cycle story had. I think its well done with its story beats, but my major qualm comes at the end of the game. Spoiler: [spoiler] I get now that the sequel is around the corner, but its kinda messed up to end your game on a to be continued screen after going through everything to defy your creator, turn the person you love against you, and hide your child away. It feels as though you ended at the rising action or the climax which left me very disappointed. [/spoiler] All in all, if you're like me and you enjoy games that will challenge you regardless of the genre, grab this game. The unique mechanics this game has made the experience enjoyable on its own.

so far im having an amazing time with this game. i really dont understand all the negative reviews. this game deserves better. i would of loved a parry in this game or being able to dash through enemies (im only 3 hours deep so maybe thats a thing later on) but overall the platforming is super fun and the combat is solid.   would 100% recommend this game for lovers of the platforming/metroidvania/souls genres.  (at the time of this review the game is only 10$ on fanatical so at that price point is a no brainer)  Edit #1: so this game is still riddled with bugs. nothing that really ruins the experience but it's worth noting. sometimes you pick up skill points and they don't register. i've also had the game freeze after i did one of the speedrun trials. one more bug i had in the light tower was my character was propelled up and then kept flying up and i couldn't do anything about it. even after i died my character would continuously fly up. i'm not really saying these things to discourage anyone but i think its important to note it.   besides that i'm having a great time. one thing i didn't mention in my initial review was that the art for the environments are really nice. the character designs aren't my favorite ever but overall the art is solid. the music is pretty great. also found out there really aren't i-frames and you can't dodge through enemies which i thought was a weird choice but you get used to fighting without them + parrying is not a thing.  i will edit again if i need to as i get further. definitely still recommend as of now.

Probably one of the best Metroidvanias I ever played, very closely after Hollow Knight (which it's kinda inspired by). The challenge is great - and never unfair since many checkpoints are scattered throughout the map. Some mechanics are pretty innovative and enrichen the game with even harder and sometimes more unforgiving platforming passages. The amount of exploration is insane and soundtrack is beautiful (Vinyl, when?). There were some occasional hickups with my two Xbox based-controllers, but I guess it's taken care of by the Devs. My only gripe is the little bit weird/unusual weapon mechanics - I wish I could rather equip than tackling with multiple buttons for different weapons - this sometimes interferes and overlaps with platforming button settings...and at times remapping is necessary. I feel original (Noctis) difficulty is perfectly paced - and it's not too hard - just not a walk in the park which should be expected with a true Metroidvania. A game which fully respects the player and doesn't sacrifice difficulty for the mainstream. Eagerly awaiting the successor in 2025. The amount of love and work put into this massive game by such a small dev team is still beyond me. Kudos!

Aeterna Noctis is a great metroidvania with a few minor, but noticeable flaws. For what appears to be the team's first game together, that is a lot better than I would normally expect - especially self-published and with as large a scale as this game has. I would recommend this game for anyone who is a casual enjoyer of metroidvanias and/or platformers with a little bit of patience, or any hardcore enjoyers of the same genres. Even at full price, the money spent vs time invested alone justifies it, but is further justified but the amount of quality content. At the time of writing this review, it is on sale for $12 USD and that is simply a steal!  Firstly, many people comment on the difficulty of the platforming. I do not normally play platforming games, but I personally felt that it was not overwhelmingly difficult. However, there is a specific upgrade that you can choose to use/not use (another jump - up to triple jump max). I also used a controller - I could not imagine the suffering stubborn KB&M users would feel attempting the harder platforming sections. I opted to use that and I did feel that I would have felt much more frustrated in mid-late game without it. There were a handful (maybe 8-12) of challenges that felt exceptionally difficult, but they were all optional if you don't care too much about 100%ing your playthrough. Even then, they are possible. In a game with hundreds of "platforming challenges" though, I feel like the balance was fair as an inexperienced platformer gamer. Rather, what was more frustrating was how it felt tedious at times repeating sections you've already went through. This is partly a problem of the density of fast travel points, though at the same time more fast travel points would have felt unnatural.  The early and mid game combat provides a nice level of difficulty, though beyond that almost all encounters, even all the bosses, are a joke, if all you care about is defeating them. Their DPS was far too low to pose a threat as I could out-heal all of it (with two exceptions). Narratively though, that kind of makes sense. At the same time, their movesets felt too fast & complex for me to attempt to dodge reliably. Many bosses are quite tanky as well, so even wailing away non-stop can sometimes take a while. Dodging all the attacks would really draw out each attempt. As a soulslike enthusiast, this was probably the most disappointing part of my play time. The player attack movesets & animations are very well done with satisfying weight & feedback.  The character art direction felt weirdly inconsistent, but to a level that feels nitpicky. The art that is presented is nice to look at, but inconsistent. The environmental art is more consistent and certainly above-average.  The game's performance was perfect, there were no crashes during my playtime, and I only ran into maybe two or three bugs. The one that stuck out the most was some player physics sometimes not behaving as intended in the one specific area that completely changes how the player physics work (staying vague to avoid spoilers). Props to the programmer(s) for getting it working as well as it did though!  The game's music is a mixed bag. There are some certified bangers, many serviceable tracks, and a couple that kind of missed the mark. I can tell a lot passion and skill went into its composition though and I hope they are out there still making more of those certified bangers!  Lastly, the game's story is serviceable, but mostly not super memorable. The beginning and middle of the main storyline was not especially gripping, HOWEVER, the world building is some of the best out there, some of the dialogue is excellent, the main character is wonderfully written, and the finale (though felt rushed) was a very impactful surprise at the end there.  I loved it.  Thank you for the game Aeternum. I will be keeping an eye on the upcoming sequel and Eden Genesis.

Wow, what a game. This is the biggest metroidvania I have ever played at around 80 hours of length and a top 5 metroidvania for sure. Much of that time was spent trying to conquer platforming challenges. I beat it on the original noctis difficulty (which is how this game should be played). I previously though that GRIME was the best effort so far to make the next best metroidvania but it was actually this game. Unfortunately, the game stuck too close to its hollow knight inspirations and copied two things from hollow knight that have absolutely no business being in a precision platformer focused metroidvania. Those two things are the cornifer map mechanic and the corpse run. People have been complaining about the platforming being too hard or the game being too hostile towards players but the truth is they wouldn't be doing so if it weren't for these two things in the game.  There are other issues as well unrelated to hollow knight that prevent this from taking the #1 spot: - There is a rare bug that can permanently prevent you from getting 100% completion by failing to register when a quest or part of a quest was completed. For me this occurred when I played the adventurers musical note, permanently blocking me from completing the associated quest. - In dream kingdom, the combination of puzzles, both in navigating the biome and solving its puzzles was simply too much, this is easily one of the worst biomes I have ever played in a metroidvania which is sad because its also one of the most beautiful biomes I've ever seen. - The game has extremely poor breadcrumbing in the cosmos biome. Due to the location of cornifer the player is breadcrumbed into first taking the hardest mini-biome of the 4 mini biomes in the cosmos biome. This has caused the entire to get criticized heavily for its design by other players. However, if you ignore the breadcrumbing and take on the other 3 challenges first, the last one becomes much more manageable and the biome much more enjoyable overall. - Aeterna mode is marked as normal while noctis is marked as hard with a skull next to it. However, this is highly misleading. Noctis is the original game and contains the platforming challenges that make this game so great while aeterna mode replaces most of it with more combat even though the combat isn't very enjoyable to begin with.  P.S: ignore any person telling you to avoid the triple jump gem in the game. These people are elitists who are upset that the devs updated the game to be hard but fair instead of being a kaizo mario. They suffered through the game as it was on release and they want you to feel their pain, they do not have your best interests in mind, this is all about protecting their ego. Play the game as it is.

The easiest way I can describe this game is that it is a precision platformer disguising itself as a metroidvania. Unlike The Messenger which is a metroidvania disguising itself as a precision platformer. I say that because at least in The Messenger you know what you're getting into in the first half of the game, so if you enjoy the platforming nature of the game then you'll enjoy it once you get full map exploration. But, in this game if you don't enjoy precision platforming, you don't know that is the core of the game until putting several hours into the game. And then it isn't even a good platformer, the hitboxes are wonky, the controls are floaty and the combat is just meh. The game looks nice and has an interesting story and wonderful soundtrack. But the bait and switch of the type of game it is and not even being an enjoyable platformer makes this a pass for me. I've tried playing it twice and this is going into my uninstall forever list.

The TLDR here is Aeterna Noctis is a very VERY good game.  If you are a fan of Hollow Knight then you'll love AN. Art style, music score, sound effects and even a few in-game mechanics are straight out of HK so you'll feel at home here.  Where these 2 games differ significantly is where HK is a brutally difficult game which punishes you for failure, AN is just as difficult but death doesn't send you miles away as there is a well needed checkpoint system. AN does have a souls-like death mechanic but its easily negated with a simple one slot charm.  Its a huge game with a massive map. I'm 40hrs in and around 75% complete so still got a little way to go.  I do have a few gripes as follows but its great to see the devs reading the reviews and remedying some of the early teething issues.  1. The map feels a little disjointed. At will likely be around 50+ hours, its huge but unlike games like HK or Ori, it lacks the interconnection with little more than a single entry point and single exit point for each area, sometimes even the same point out as in.  2. Metroidvania gating could do with some work. Again, at 50+ hours of game time, it doesn't need to be 'padded out' but the vast majority of areas allow for 95% completion only to be thwarted in the last room where you find out you a are missing a key skill or ability. Personally, I would have preferred to have been stopped in my tracks a little sooner on my travels rather than have to back track though an entire area to resume my search.  3. The arrow / celestial shot abilities are frustratingly difficult to master. If you've played either of the Ori games, think of Bash but here the left stick feels overly sensitive. You will eventually master it but you'll find aiming a constant challenge.  4. When you eventually make it to a late game area called 'Cosmos' you'll have you're head in your hands with frustration. While its probably one of the most uniquely designed platformer levels I've ever see, when up is down, down is up, left is right and right is left you'll be screaming! Oh and then it will want you to complete a tricky platforming challenge as well.  In summary, this is an incredible game and one of the best precision platformers out there. If you struggled with the Path of Pain in HK then give AN a bash. Its just as difficult (if not more so) BUT the check point system and lack of any punishment on death allows you to die > try > die > try > die > try x 100 and you'll eventually get it.  Great work to the Devs - Will keeping an eye out for more from you guys.

Can I recommend this game? Yes. But only for a very specific crowd of people. It's a game that is very fun, even if its flaws make it fall a little short of a masterpiece of the genre.   So the good. If you like precision platforming, intense boss rights and quite satisfying movement mechanics, you'll get a kick out of this game. If you enjoy lots and lots of exploration, collections, backtracking and hunting things down in a metroidvania, you'll be happy with this. The game even lets you pay to see if you've missed anything in a zone, which is a huge boon for some of the more sprawling ones. The art is quite lovely, the sound design is solid and the game mostly runs quite well.  The bad though. First of all, there's a lot of screen clutter. The art is nice, but the visual design is often lacking. In many areas you're zoomed out very far, there's a color filter on the screen and all the enemies are very similar colors, which makes everything into visual soup, and it's hard to know where you are at a glance. One magma themed area in particular struggled with this, everything was just a mass of red, even your character. This gets worse in boss fights, when the screen fills with lights and colors and particles, some of which matter and some don't, and it can be intensely troublesome to see what you're doing. There are a few glitches, some of which come up a lot, that lock you out of movement or give you weird animations. None of it gamebreaking, but certainly immersion breaking. None of this ruins the game, but it all adds up to helping it fall a little short of perfect for me.   Overall though, if you like your metroidvanias with a very solid dose of precision platforming, go right ahead and indulge in this, and you won't regret it.

So, this is a weird one. Because actually, I thought this game was actually really great, in most ways.  tl;DR: I will say, despite the rant below, I don't hate this game at all, I hate certain aspects of it that drag it down, and make the rest of the game worse for it. There's some great boss fights (brought down for me by the no-hit achievements for every one of them), great platforming sections (brought down by them being almost pointlessly long, and the game almost always making you manually backtrack the way you came in said overly long platforming sections), some great ideas with moving around and traversing quickly (brought down by terrible, convoluted controls that did not need to be twinstick aiming for 99% of the game). Pretty solid music and art and world building, lovely little cutscenes, and the story was fine. So yeah. It's impossible for me to recommend this game because it's a 20h game turned into a 50h slog because of the design decisions. You might still enjoy it though; I did.... half of the time.  RANT FOLLOWS:  Rarely have I played a game with so much potential, crippled by terrible design choices by the devs. The controls in this game are absolutely -terrible-. It's baffling how bad they are. You press a face button on your controller to fire arrows, holding it down, and then aim with the left stick. This is terrible. It'd still be terrible if it was right stick, though, you know why? Because there's literally not one shot you have to make with the arrows (to hit switches, and later, to teleport) IN THE ENTIRE GAME that requires twinstick aiming. The only exception is versus two of the bosses of the game, one is a secret optional fight, the other is the final boss. Now you might say, well, just rebind them. Oh, I assure you I did. But, it's worse. There are multiple arrow types, which you have to switch between, and one of those two arrow types itself has another sub-type (dark arrows), which is switched with yet another different button. And the teleport arrows, once you switch to them, require a FOURTH button to then teleport to the arrow while it is in mid-flight. This feels terrible from the moment you gain the ability. Here's an idea: have the teleport arrows simply be an upgrade to your regular arrows, removing the need to switch to them. Then, when you fire the arrow, merely pressing the arrow button again will teleport you. There. I just replaced FOUR separate buttons  with one button, the same button. What about the alternate dark arrows? Who cares. I literally never used them, because they're terrible. And if you really must have multiple arrows, then just have one button to fire, and one to switch arrows. Not four different buttons AND your left stick to mess around with arrows. Just stops the games momentum dead in its tracks, every time you use arrows. And, as for holding down the button to aim, that can go away too, and be replaced with 8-way aiming, like Contra or a million other games that work totally fine without twinstick aiming. I understand these were probably design decisions for variety and yada yada yada I don't care. It sucks. It sucks so bad, it made me just constantly cringe at how it tanked enjoyment of the game for me, and I'm not the only one, looking at other reviews and forum posts.  And then there's the achievement design. This is a game with tough boss fights, with screen filling attacks, bullet hell waves of projectiles, super fast counters, etc. That's fine, I like challenge. What I do *not* like is BEATING EVERY BOSS NO HIT. Especially, when the boss design is pretty much designed to beat you absolutely bloody no matter how good you are dodging. I understand they're all possible to be beaten no-hit. I'm not saying it's not possible. But I am saying that YOU, or at least 99.99% of anyone reading this, will not no-hit them all. You'll see why if you play the game. Some of these fights are absurd to no-hit, with so many cheap attacks and multiple forms. Again, I *love* tough boss fights. But if your boss can be memorized to the point that you can no-hit it, that's actually boring and terrible boss design to me. It should be a struggle, it should be barely won. You know, like these fights. Except they still expect you to beat them flawlessly... wtf were they thinking??  So, you might be thinking, ok, the bosses are messed up, the controls are messed up, but, isn't this game all about the platforming? Yeah, except, you can't platform nearly as well with an analog stick, and because of their awful, poorly thought out control scheme, you need to use the analog stick to move, since every button on the controller is already used. And besides, you need the left stick for aiming the arrows too. Oh look, another crippling game flaw that would've been neatly sidestepped with better controls, and 8-way aiming, removing the need for the god-awful left-stick aiming. Not only that, the platforming sections, which also should've been tons of fun, are made to be as excruciating as possible in every way.... with every long difficult section that ends in a dead end (with a power-up or switch or whatever) requiring you to then do the section backwards to get out of it. If you hit the spikes, 98% of the time you get respawned at the end of the dead-end, and have to get back out again the hard way. I think this would've been fine for some areas, particularly some optional challenges. But doing it basically every. Single. Time...... it's so obnoxious, it just feels like the devs don't respect the player's time, and just want to jack up the playtime as much as possible. All in the name of it being "ohhhhh so hardcore, we're the most hardcore difficult metroidvania!" I hate to break it to you guys, making obnoxious crap that's just poor design, and to waste people's time, doesn't make your game the most hardcore or difficult. It's neither the hardest bosses nor the hardest platforming I've ever played in a metroidvania, so get over yourselves.

Что ж. Потрачено 30 часов, проходилось на обычной сложности. Общий прогресс 86%/100%, мне в конце уже было откровенно лениво искать абсолютно все зеркала и сундуки.   Это мой второй заход в игру, в первом мне обломали зубы и клешни завышенной сложностью платформинга, что при неадекватно огромных локациях превращалось в гребаный ад. Взяла год спустя игру на распродаже, сразу запустила на изи-моде - и хосспаде, оказывается этерна нормальная игра!  Мне очень нравится:  + визуальный стиль и музыка, локации красивые и необычные (бездна и море отвал всего).  + персонажи, саркастичность гг - то, что поднимало мне настроение.  + история вроде бы выглядит как избитая сказка, но есть нюансики, и из-за них в конце взгрустнулось (а совсем в конце захотелось кое-кому задать вопросики и набить морду) + прокачка и возможность сбросить древо не используя расходники + персонаж управляется плавно и отзывчиво + интересная механика кристальных стрел-телепортов.  Перечислю минусы, а то кажется что от игры я в восторге (это не так): - неадекватно огромная карта. тут или надо было больше тп-тронов насовать (в локации космос же нормально получилось!), или уменьшить размеры этих локаций.  - при всех красивостях визуала объекты переднего плана перекрывают врагов и деспавнящие гг шипы, в последней локе это особенно больно.  - бестиарий просто счетчик убитых мобов и их виды. без описаний и лора. зачем оно тогда?  - длиннющие платформинговые секции с вкусняхой в конце. через такую-то матерь добрался? а теперь топай назад тем же путем, шортката не существует.  - журнал квестов гребаная шутка. поговорил с каким-то дедом и забыл что он там просил сделать? ну лол, ходи гадай теперь, сам виноват что не держишь ручку с листочком под рукой.  - 70% камней или абсолютно бесполезны, или даются настолько поздно по игре, что уже не важно (да-да, практически все красные камни, я на вас смотрю).  - на пк игрок может напрочь забыть про существование сюрикенов, копья, катаны и топора. почему-то кнопки их действий при полной банке крови не вызывают их (лол). однако вообще не вызывает никаких проблем их отсутствие (дважды лол). - гринд монеток. на мелочи жизни вроде карт и покупок кусочков хп хватит всегда, а вот атлас и покупка нот за 100к(!!!!!!) это уже за гранью добра и зла. - сюда же гринд экспы.  - тут используется набившая оскомину механика из дс - если помер, иди подбирай душу. слава бездне есть камень это отключающий, с ним и ходила всю игру. реально упрощает жизнь.   По итогу - добротная метроидвания с хорошим сюжетом и шикарным визуальным стилем и музыкой. Рекомендую ознакомиться, если сильно сомневаетесь - берите по скидке.

This game is really good, at first.  The platforming is hard but still doable, and the combat is fun. Your basic attack is a little bit shorter range than in most metroidvanias, but it's not by much, and I got used to it quickly, and most enemies have attack patterns where the shorter range is fine.  Then, about 8-9 hours into the game, I unlocked the teleport arrow, where you fire an arrow and can then teleport to it. And the game started to focus on it. Heavily.  The problem is that the controls for it are absolutely terrible. You have to aim it at very specific angles that aren't even increments of 45 degrees, while in midair and falling. It slows time while you're aiming, but not by much, and the arrow will instantly fire after a few seconds of aiming. There's upgrades you can take that make it slow time more, but it requires investing almost all your skill points into a side of the tree that focuses on arrows in general for combat, and I already hate using the bow, so spending my points to make them take over even more of the game is not an appealing prospect.  At time of writing, I'm about 12 hours in. I'm still enjoying some parts of the game, but I'm reaching the point where about three quarters of my playtime is spent on extremely precise platforming puzzles with the teleport arrow where I have to fire off multiple ones at incredibly unforgiving angles within the space of a couple of seconds, and if I mess up at any point in this 15-second sequence of these teleports, I have to start over.  It sucks. And it's frustrating, because until the developers decided to focus so heavily on that mechanic, I was thinking this might be one of my favorite games of all time. But the further I press on, the more and more this stupid teleport arrow is taking over the game, and I hate it. And I LOVE hard platforming in most games, but in this case it's hard because of bad controls.  But I got the game on sale for like 15 bucks, and I got 8-10 hours of very high quality fun out of it, and most games don't come close to that, so I still recommend it. But just be aware that the game may wear out its welcome for you.  EDIT: I stuck it out and got through the arrow area. The next area didn't rely on it so heavily, but it introduced an even worse mechanic: invisible walls, invisible enemies, invisible ceilings, and invisible spikes that are only visible while an arrow is near them, coupled with doors that aren't linked and teleport you to different areas. The map is completely useless, and you essentially have to memorize each room's layout via trial and error unless you want to move at a snail's pace as you unveil three steps ahead of you with each arrow.  I think I'm done with the game. It was great at first, but after a while it just started to come up with the worst possible ideas that removed the fun.

Sunk almost 60 hours in to fully complete and enjoyed every minute. Absolutely solid Metroidvania. Has just enough new and interesting mechanical tweaks to make it feel unique, whilst also having a familiar mechanical feel to games like Hollow Knight. Great art style, great soundtrack and great game play.  It's definitely more challenging than some of the other Metroidvanias out there. The platforming needs pixel-perfect timing at points and mastering the crystal arrows takes practice, but damn you feel good perfecting the mechanics. Boss fights range in difficulty from medium to brutal, but a lot of the end-game boss fights can be brute-forced with an increased health pool and the right gem set-ups.  My only minor criticisms would be some of the localisation grammatical errors/oversights, which can take you out of the game a little. Not having an "item menu" to see quest items you've picked up also makes keeping track of progress a little difficult sometimes. Did notice some FPS drops in locations too, but usually only brief and could be ignored.  Would 100% recommend.

This is one of those things where I wish there was a middle option for a game.   So Firstly The good stuff. The game is well crafted, the art is amazing, and soundtrack is fantastic. The combat (at first) is pretty great, has a good amount of different weapons that you can use, along with some accessories (gems) and talent points to really have some cool customiziable builds. Also this game is huge for a metroidvania, you will definitely require a good amount of hours to explore it, even without going for 100% exploration. There are a good amount side quests, tons of characters to meet, plenty of enemy variety throughout the worlds.   The main reason that I am saying no, is because i don't think that this game presents itself as a precision platformer from the start, but becomes one for the later half of the game. Sure platforming is there from the start, but it feels fairly moderate, and the game has a nice mix of combat, exploration, and platforming. However the second half of the game totally shifts pace and becomes overwhelmingly a precision platformer, akin to like celeste, meat boy, or hollow knights path of pain section. This makes exploration an a tedious chore, especially when backtracking around zones looking for secrets. This game would have MASSIVELY benefited from having switches after completing sections to turn off those puzzles so you could backtrack and not have it be an absolute slog. On top of it, the hit boxes for spikes and instant death zones (that are literally everywhere) felt larger than the actual displayed spikes causing annoying deaths constantly during long complex sections. Also Half of the platforming in the game involves using an ability where you teleport to an arrow you shoot, which is cool in theory however trying to do precision aiming on a joystick never feels great. I feel like i'm fighting the controls so often just trying to aim where i want. This was especially awful for me during the cosmos/ space section of the game. For me, it was NOT enjoyable for the controls to be constantly flipping around on you especially when you're talking about doing platforming sections that are as tight and difficult as these ones are. The only saving grace was the abundance of checkpoints everywhere because I would NOT have finished this game if they weren't everywhere.  Boss fights also become tedious in my opinion. Boss attack patterns become pretty complex and i found that some of the later in the bosses just teleport around the room constantly and 98% of the fights are just spent dodging mechanics and only getting in a few hits in the short windows when they go into a rest animation for a second or so.  So if You are a fan of precision platforming, and enjoy those types of brutally hard segments then you probably will enjoy this. But if you're looking for something with a nice mix like i was, the second half of this game will absolutely be a frustrating slog and you should avoid it.

Best metroidvania since Hollow Knight or Grime. This game starts slow like all metroidvanias, but after a couple hours things begin to click. The entire game is a testament to the dedication of Aeternum Game Studios in pursuing excellence. Difficulty is similar to the end game content of Hollow Knight. It has a modern Castlevania feel like Bloodstained: ROTN. I read the mixed reviews on here and viewed a few in-depth YouTube reviews before purchasing. This is one of the best Metroidvanias, mechanically, of all time. The story and lore are good enough to give the player purpose, the art is beautiful at times and frustrating at others, enemy AI is clunky and predictable, the world is a bit too complex, but the platforming and movement is in a tier that few games reside in; and that's what Metroidvanias are all about, is it not?  You'll find areas throughout in the game that continue to offer fresh takes on platforming and precision movement. The formula doesn't get stale, and each challenge feels rewarding to complete. There is little reprieve, so the game does feel exhausting at times, so don't walk in expecting bursts of tough interlaced with periods of power; your skills are constantly being tested with little reprieve.   Two major complaints:  -The art style is not my favorite, but that is purely preference. The issue with the art itself is that the environment is often too busy and cluttered, hiding information from the player. Not ideal for a metroidvania. -It took me a couple tries to get into the game due to lack of direction and immediate complexity. It's easy to get lost when you first start, and many of the game's systems are thrown at you at the onset. It's certainly overwhelming.  One major point of praise: -The uniqueness and design of the platforming challenges are equal to, if not better than, Celeste and Hollow Knight. If you play Metroidvanias for the precision movement and difficult platforming, I'd say Aeterna Noctis is the category of "must play".

This game is a solid 8.5/10, definitely underrated and I don't really understand why, but I would imagine it has to do with the difficulty of the platforming in certain sections of the game.  It takes a lot of the best elements that are common from the metroidvania genre, but it is unique enough to set it aside from the rest. For example, there are certain traversal skills that can be acquired in any particular order if you are good enough at platforming, and even unique achievements for doing this.  If you enjoyed Hollow Knight you will definitely see some similarities in terms of movement/combat/graphics, but that isn't necessarily a bad thing. Hollow Knight was just the trend setter.   One thing that has to be said about this one is that it is tough as nails. Both the combat and platforming can be incredibly unforgiving at times. About 1/2 of the way through the game you get a traversal skill in the form of an arrow that you aim which you can then teleport to (think of the skill in symphony of the night, but requiring more expertise), and the game will FORCE you to perfect this skill.  At times I had to put the game down because I got so frustrated at how reliant the game was on making you utilize this skill for movement, especially near the endgame, and particularly if you want to find everything on the map. I just kept hoping for something that would make it easier to use or replace it, but that never happens.   Overall, I highly recommend this one, but only if you enjoy a good challenge. I played on hard mode (noctis) which is the 'intended' way to play, but I'm sure it's still good on the easier difficulty.

仅适合有一定动作游戏基础的平台跳跃爱好者游玩——这并不是因为游戏的平台跳跃内容量多且平均难度较大，而是因为这游戏除了这之外就没有堪称优秀的地方了：  没有丝毫的地图设计，仅靠一条直线把地图连接起来； 在没有多少RPG元素的情况下，该游戏的数值设计还能在中后期彻底崩坏，正常收集打BOSS得打十几分钟，实在令我佩服； BOSS的技能设计很新颖，演出效果很不错，但是完全没有考虑BOSS的难度与合理性。有的BOSS全程不给鲜血，有的BOSS几乎没有任何攻击机会，只能在它攻击时来几下。这就导致你费尽心力躲技能不如把技能点全点中间，然后疯狂换血，实属畸形； 小怪设计更是一点不动脑子，前期小怪打起来是回合制，后期小怪技能已经满天飞了，完全没有让人打的欲望； 商店卖的东西有多贵也不用多说了，快一百万才能把游戏的货品清空，一个宝箱就给几百几千金币真的可笑； 手绘风格的画面可圈可点，但是又喜欢给你搞光污染，点名高塔、熔炉区域； 弓箭的键鼠操作甚至不显示鼠标指针，配合上宇宙区域操作感拉满； ………………………………  总之如果你不喜欢平台跳跃，这款游戏就是超级大粪作，千万别买

Aeterna Noctis is, without a doubt, one of the finest indie games I've ever played.  It's a must-play for fans of Metroidvanias and people who want to experience the genre, and exemplifies the formula with an original and compelling world.  Bursting with satisfying challenges of skill, reflex, and observation, at no point did I feel mastery was out of reach with enough practice.  I loved the visuals, the music, the build variety, and just about everything.  What can I say about it negatively?  The game has a handful of bugs that may impede play.  The script isn't perfect English and occasionally lacks punctuation.  Filling out your map is sometimes a chore because in certain areas, you need to be physically present in every corner of the room.  I can see how the endgame zones can be confusing to navigate.  Other critiques have been made, many smacking of blatant skill issue and severe cases of butthurt and hyperbole, but the developers have continued to listen and support their game.  Ya gotta give it up.  This game ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ rules.

This game is amazing. Probably the best metroidvania I've ever played at the time of this review. The sheer amount of content you get for the price tag, especially when on sale, is enormous. Not to mention the artwork is beautiful, the visuals and mechanics of the boss designs (for the most part) are some of the best I've ever seen, the skill tree progression is neat to have, the tremendous amount of passive skill customizations through gems is enormous, there are many traversal abilities unlocked during the playthrough that tremendously change gameplay and unlock new areas, the maps are very cleverly designed, and the precision platforming (on Noctis difficulty) is some of the craziest I've ever seen. To top it off, you can switch the difficulty on the fly if at any point you don't feel up for the crazy platform challenges on Noctis difficulty or just want to backtrack quickly. However, I did notice a strange bug in the endgame area (I believe it's called Royal Stairway) though - after dying, the game wouldn't accept any of my movement inputs and I was stuck at the lantern/throne. I haven't had any issues with losing progress on my map or saves as reported in some of the negative reviews.  Side note: At the current time of review, I basically shot straight through the story and collected all the shards and ran into a roadblock with the High Aurora boss fight. I will come back and finish the endgame once I generally make more progress and become stronger first.

It seems like they made this game difficult just for the sake of being difficult.  I can appreciate a good challenge, but most of the time this game felt very largely unrewarding and a waste of time.  One of my first impressions was that it felt like they were trying to incorporate a lot of aspects of Hollow Knight, without really understanding what made Hollow Knight fun.  The controls, hit boxes, etc. are too inconsistent to feel good as a precision platformer.  Despite all of that, I probably could have kept powering through to the end if it weren't for the horrible level design.  Between the excessively long, repetitive platforming sections, the backtracking, and the general poor direction from the game narrative, it became quite the slog.  Eventually, I just decided it wasn't worth it.  It's a little sad.  I don't think there was really much with art or music direction that I had issue with.  It seems like it actually could have been a pretty good game if it weren't for some unfortunate design choices and maybe lack of game testing (or willingness to put out a game with flawed mechanics).

Muito bom! Esse jogo é extremamente subestimado, ainda não tem a fama que deveria.   Existem vários cenários diferentes um do outro e todos são muito muito bonitos, o único problema que encontrei é que tem alguns que falta contraste e é difícil algumas partículas. Mesmo assim é bem divertido explorar todos eles.  O jogo tem uma dificuldade meio alta, principalmente jogando na dificuldade Noctis, as plataformas são bem desafiadoras. O único defeito na dificuldade é que os bosses não são tão difíceis assim, então a maior parte da dificuldade acaba sendo em acessar os lugares e ir pulando as plataformas.  Os itens e habilidades também são bem legais, dá pra usar várias builds diferentes e cada habilidade que vc consegue, muda muito o jogo e facilita muito explorar novos lugares.   Pra falar alguns pontos negativos, achei o design de personagens bem fraco, principalmente se comparar com o cenário. A história não é grande coisa, bem básica.   De qualquer forma, com certeza compensa testar esse jogo, existe muito tempo de conteúdo e se vc gosta de jogos tipo Hollow Knight, com certeza vale a pena jogar Aeterna.

I have to admit that I vacillated a little bit about whether or not to recommend this game.  I spent quite a bit of the time I played this game really quite frustrated.  I play a lot of games, generally consider myself pretty skillful of them, and yet still had quite a few times that I just thought about putting it down and leaving it alone.  Having played through the game and its extended content, however, I clearly enjoyed it enough to keep pushing through, so I will give this a qualified review: get it if you A) want to challenge yourself; B) want a *large* metroidvania - it took me 45ish hours; and C) don't mind beating your head against the wall.

Aeterna Noctis es un juego recomendable, una linda experiencia que por momentos es un poco frustrante pero no por su aciertos en la dificultad, sino por errores en la programación. Todo lo bueno se puede reducir en que tiene una historia simple pero linda, personajes con personalidad, una dirección de arte que no resalta como novedosa pero hace bien lo que se propone y por sobre todo una jugabilidad que se siente muy fluida. La variedad de enemigos es la suficiente como para no aburrirse aunque hay veces que uno apetece saltearlos sin más por ser un poco repetitivo (más que nada al final del juego). Para que tengan una persectiva, yo use el doble salto en cuanto tuve la posibilidad y nunca me saqué el triple salto, por lo que la dificultad de las plataformas se redujeron bastante para mi partida. Esto porque no es un metroidvania con plataformas, es un juego de plataformas con elementos de rpg y metroidvania, no es malo para nada, pero es lo que es. No completé tantas sidequest por lo que quizá me faltó algo de la experiencia, pero es completa y me sorprende que sea el primer juego de este estudio, si pulen las cosas la continuación va a ser impresionante. Lo malo y por lo que quizá no lo recomendaría a menos que te guste mucho este tipo de juegos es que tiene algunos bug bastante molestos (que no te muestran bien al personaje) o algunas partes el cuerpo de daño difiere con lo que ves (especialmente molesto en algunas plataformas muy precisas que no tocas pero te dañan.  Le doy 7/10 aunque si tengo en cuenta que es un grupo pequeño le subiría a 8/10. Ojalá sigan así en la próxima.  -  Aeterna Noctis is a recommended game, a nice experience that at times is a bit frustrating not because of its accuracy in difficulty, but due to errors in programming. All the good can be summed up in that it has a simple but nice story, characters with personality, an art direction that doesn't stand out as innovative but does well what it sets out to do, and above all, gameplay that feels very fluid. The variety of enemies is enough to keep you from getting bored, although there are times when you feel like skipping them because they're a bit repetitive (mostly towards the end of the game). To give you some perspective, I used the double jump as soon as I had the chance and never took off the triple jump, so the difficulty of the platforms was greatly reduced for my playthrough. This is because it's not a metroidvania with platforming, it's a platforming game with RPG and metroidvania elements; it's not bad at all, but it is what it is. I didn't complete many side quests, so maybe I missed out on some of the experience, but it's comprehensive, and it surprises me that this is the first game from this studio. If they polish things up, the sequel will be impressive. The downside, and why I might not recommend it unless you really like this type of game, is that it has some quite annoying bugs (that don't display the character properly) or some parts where the hitbox doesn't match what you see (especially annoying on some very precise platforms that you don't touch but still get damaged). I give it a 7/10, although if I take into account that it's a small group, I'd raise it to an 8/10. I hope they continue like this in the next one.

Noctis Mode (no Vertigo gem): 109%, all simulations gold  To get to the point, challenging precision platforming is the singular aspect of the game that I believe to be noteworthy. From the beginning of the game, before you unlock any additional abilities and are limited to a dash and grounded jump, it’s clear that the game will be demanding more from you than your typical metroidvania. Levels are designed such that there’s relatively little lenience when it comes to even the most basic of platforming. Basic traversal does get more forgiving when you unlock more abilities, but the difficulty is shifted to dedicated platforming sections, especially the optional challenges. There’s a nice difficulty curve, but by about halfway through the game, you’ll already be doing Path of Pain-tier platforming more often than not. This obviously isn’t for everyone, but if it sounds appealing to you, Aeterna Noctis really does deliver on that front.  Unfortunately, this is where my praise ends.  My main gripe is with Aeterna Noctis is its visuals. Visual clarity is often poor and occasionally downright unacceptable, and that’s hard to overlook considering its direct effect on gameplay. Not only are platforming elements often hard to distinguish from the background, but the visual clutter from all the bloom and particle effects only exacerbates the problem. In this regard, the game is one of the worst I’ve seen in a platformer. On top of that, stiff animations and minimal visual feedback for certain mechanics are issues as well.  Furthermore, the game’s art direction is non-existent. The artstyle is inconsistent, with individual models, backgrounds, effects, portraits, and cutscenes all having different styles. There’s a constant visual clash. I also think some elements are outright embarrassingly amateurish and ugly (to the point where I cannot believe they’re in the game), but I admit that’s more a matter of taste, and I’d be willing to forgive it if it were part of a cohesive vision and not an unholy amalgamation of styles that evokes Walmart Hollow Knight at best and Deviant Art at worst. Aeterna Noctis looks like a Flash game in 4K in the worst possible way.  Moreover, the is not a particularly “good” metroidvania. By that I mean its critical progression felt relatively linear. It’s open in that you can tackle individual areas in different orders, especially in the front half of the game, and achievements outright acknowledge these sequence breaks (though I use this term lightly). For example, there are achievements for defeating certain bosses before getting the light arrows, teleport arrows, and double jump respectively. I got two of these three, but I never felt like I did any special platforming to bypass soft gates and do content in an interesting order. I just completed whichever self-contained area I came across.  And that’s just how most of the game felt, especially in the back half. You enter a big, vaguely squared-shaped area for the first time, and complete it without having to leave. Sure, you might have the option to return later for quests and optional power-ups, but this is the most shallow form of non-linear progression. I’d much rather have the critical path bounce me back and forth between areas, with optional stuff being made available to be gradually and organically. Aeterna Noctis doesn't do this at all.  Here's a lightning round of other criticisms I have of the game: unbalanced combat and wildly inconsistent boss quality with very low lows, generally tedious area design with some areas being exceptionally bloated, poorly designed quests, shockingly unclear UI (most offensive being how little of the map you can see at once), and just a little bit of glitchiness as a cherry on top.  I’m really torn on this game, but it's probably worth your time if you're into precision platforming and somehow aren't turned off by the visuals. I feel like I got my money's worth, but I have a hard time praising this game as the masterpiece that it could've been with a little more time in the oven.

Из-за рождения ребенка пришлось бросить данную игру после 22 часов прохождения. Но вот спустя 2 года я решил таки вернуться к данному творению, т.к. ну что-то прям манило манило в ней. Игра отличная, играется очень хорошо и приятно, если делаешь всё правильно в платформенных секциях. И при этом она очень и очень продолжительная, при этом это ни разу не минус. Наоборот - играть приятно и заканчивать не хочется. Даже сейчас после 53 часов игры, за которые я прошёл её на 109 %, собрав и сделав всё, даже как-то грустно что ли, что она закончилась. В конце конечно очень уж душный местами начался платформинг, а иногда и сплошняком шла душнина. Хотелось сдаться и всё бросить, хотелось кого-нибудь убить, нооо почему-то это только подстёгивало наоборот её пройти. Игра даёт вызов! И ещё какой. При этом настолько грамотно всё сделано, что каждый раз игра пробивает некий потолок сложности, и ты думаешь "Да блин это самое сложное место, как они это придумали". А потом ещё такое место и ещё..И бывшими когда-то для тебя сложные места уже выглядят как простейший элемент. А после 4-ой печати императора вообще всё стало побоку в этой игре )))) И это очень хорошо, когда прогрессия в игре по сути зависит только от тебя! Да, в игре есть и прокачка, и некоторые вещи немного могут упростить платформенный геймплей, но всё-таки главное это твоя голова! Игра учит терпению и спокойствию что ли, а не просто идти по указке! И это очень важно, т.к. современные игры стали какими-то пустышками, не несущих ничего полезного.   Единственным недостатком разве что можно назвать недоработанность мира, в частности локаций - перемещений между  ними и по ним. Между регионами практически нет связок, всегда один вход в неё и всё. И не хватало какого-то первооткрывательства что ли, когда наполучал способностей и можешь попасть туда именно из-за своей смекалки. Тут же всё построенно именно на платформинге. Но зато есть в плане дизайна интересные оригинальные решения, местами даже удивили.  Игру очень рекомендую, если не пугает сложный платформинг! Даже если пугает, то по сюжету именно сверхсложного платформинга нет, да и добавили режим сложности попроще, по большей части влияющий именно на платформинг.

I originally bought Aeterna Noctis because it was on sale and I heard it was a pretty extensive game.  The reviews were fairly mixed, where many people enjoyed it while the detractors generally said it was unfair, unbalanced, etc.  I am so glad I gave this game the time it deserves because it's turning out to be one of my favorite games of the genre.  It's an absolute triumph in just about every regard.  The level of detail in the worlds is gorgeous and the gameplay is very fair, even with a high degree of toughness.  When I look for a game, I want something that will continually introduce new gameplay mechanics and use those mechanics to intelligently to force me to hone my skills, while preventing frustration.  I was reminded of the classic, Super Meat Boy as I played Aeterna Noctis due to the instant restarts after death and the constant feeling of thinking "just one more try."  It's also rare in a game that I feel so motivated to hunt down every collectible and item.  Usually, games present a multitude of options but no necessity to use them since the enemy and level/puzzle designs  don't require much thought.  Aeterna Noctis constantly forced me to use all my tools and made searching for every item extremely fun since this process usually requires backtracking to extremely tricky regions of previous areas.  For me, this game shines and absolutely deserves to be placed with Hollow Knight as one of the best Metroidvanias available.  This game just gets better and better as unique challenges are revealed and always made my jaw drop.

I started on Hard and I am going to keep playing on Hard to finish this game eventually, but this bit needs to  be said;  This game's design is absolute garbage.  EDIT: Oh wait, wait! There's more! On top of my rant below I realized after the fact that I had become completely desensitized to how much excess effects and foreground objects are on the screen while you're playing a precision platformer and hack'n'slash game. Especially the Phoenix boss that actually confused me as to how a gigantic flame splash was not an actual attack that could hit me but just fluff, and bullets were obscured by a bunch of particle effects with no meaning to them. Seriously who the hell thinks looking good comes before being playable? Becoming disturbingly used to it makes me want to cry - even Hollow Knight does this, but not to this extent, and that was the worst part of that game to begin with.  1. Every single pathway to an item (or a completely useless chest filled with money, which there are plenty of to really piss you off) will ALWAYS force you to just go back the same difficult path back. This is stupid and tedious, especially if whatever was at the end of a jumping gauntlet was worthless. The alternative is using a warp potion, but then you'll end up a long long way back at a save point so that's not really an option if you want to keep progressing, so you simply are required to do every single jumping hell twice, or even more. This is especially aggravating padding since the game already uses teleports to move you back to an exit, but only sometimes, and doesn't even do it consistently enough so that you'd know where to have them.  2. There is too much of this game, and that's an amazing achievement; The maps are so massive that you actually get sick of it, this is many times bigger than Hollow Knight, for example, but most of the empty space is just a long tedious corridor that then ends in a "can't go this way" skill block. This even happens at the end of difficult jumping gauntlets so it makes you want to snap your controller in half.  3. The game's buggy, absolutely unplayable at parts, especially with the harder 'advanced' version of the bosses. I have had my attack buttons all suddenly stop working and all I could do was die and retry the boss. I've had the slow-motion gem (giving you more time to react when using teleportation arrows) suddenly just keep slowing the game down endlessly in one particular boss attack, consistently, every single time I did it. Seemed that pausing and unpausing the game fixed this but do you really want to start having to pause-buffer during a boss to fix a glitch the devs don't seem intent on fixing?  4. I need to point out first that I play Touhou Lunatics for funsies - this game's screen is a lot more cluttered than that and taking damage isn't optional anymore, it will happen because there's so much stuff on the screen, AND the screen consistently shakes non-stop while the screen has several needle-♥♥♥♥ attacks flying around that you're supposed to be aware of. It's torture on the eyes.  5. The game wastes your time thinking it's ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ funny. One questline has you hear from a terminal you can't use it without admin permission, so you beat an admin boss, go back to the terminal, terminal tells you the card needs charging, so you beat a repeat of another boss, you charge the card, get back to a terminal and update, you go back to a different terminal to open a door, it tells you you need to fight the admin boss' hard version again (during which most of my bugs with the controls happened) and you go back to the door to get a +50% arrow charge speed gem (which you likely don't want because arrows are absolute ass in this game better used for movement than combat except in rare instances). It's tedious and aggravating when the gem could just instead be placed in any single number of challenges or pathways that ARE INSTEAD DEAD ENDS WITH A ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ USELESS CHEST.  The other negative reviews are absolutely on point: This game suffers from horrendous design, the challenge is the little things, the little annoyances that keep clawing at your ball sack every time they cause you to take damage, not the actual jumping or fighting. The design of the game is making everything much harder than it really is, and that is so far removed from what makes Hollow Knight a challenging game that it's laughable to compare the two at that point. I swear on my life that even something as simple as doing a double jump out of a teleport is a die roll because it doesn't consistently work properly, and I was starting to suspect a broken controller if it wasn't for the fact that every other game works fine with it.  How is it possible that someone put this much work and effort into a game but they all agreed that this gameplay design was acceptable and not the antithesis to challenge and fun?   I'll give the game this though: Some people have complained that the teleportation arrows and the aiming are a pain, but I thought it was easy enough to align yourself to do just straight shots to always have them work the way you wanted. But that's about it. Their use in the platforming still feels horrendous so I don't blame anyone for hating that part as well.

This game is AMAZING. I am seeing negative reviews focused on one of two things that have both been resolved by these dedicated devs  Difficulty complaints - There is an optional aeterna mode which tunes down the difficulty. This is hand designed to still curate a great metroidvania experience EasyAntiCheat complaints - The devs listened to the feedback and COMPLETELY REMOVED THE ANTICHEAT. This is amazing, it shows the devs listen and that they care enough to take back a change to ensure the community is happy.  Despite all the negative reviews, there is truly only one issue that I have and it is a common issue among many games... The default controls SUCK. Luckily, this game allows you the ability to fully rebind your controls and once I found the perfect control scheme for it, it felt amazing.   I played with controller and before I go further let me just explain my control scheme with dualshock controller button references. I took advantage of the fact that you could bind multiple actions to the same button so some buttons could share actions without an overlapping issue.   The main actions: Sword attack - R1 Jump - X Dash - Circle Interact - Triangle Healing (both potion AND spell) - dpad down [spoiler] Scythe/Spear attack [/spoiler] - R2 [spoiler] Katana attack [/spoiler] - square [spoiler] Dark shield [/spoiler] - L1 HERE IS THE BIG ONE [spoiler] arrow AND arrow-freeze [/spoiler] - L2 [spoiler] arrow action (teleport) [/spoiler] - square [spoiler] The arrow is notoriously the MOST difficult part of the game to get used to. The default controls don't help as they make things way too complicated. By doing this, you are able to prime and shoot the arrow with just your left hand, and then teleport by pressing square. This feels very natural with you already using x to jump and circle to dash in platforming segments. [/spoiler]  I covered most of it, I have other binding for the remaining controls that feel good but really those are less important.   Now, the actual game is incredible. I found the story to be pretty generic anime plot at the start, but as you continue the game and get more story beats it starts unfolding and you get a very cool understanding of the narrative. The motivations of the main character become more clear and this goes from [spoiler]being a generic any-cycle to being the most important cycle of all time.[/spoiler]  The biomes are diverse and each one feels unique. The early biomes don't stand out too much as they are things we have seen in other games. (graveyard, tower, goblin caves) What is really cool is the biomes in the second half of the map. What is cool to me may be hair tearing frustration for others but in the second half we see really amazing and unique environments that make full use of the move abilities that are unlocked in the first half.  [spoiler] Robot biome (good music and very different theme wise to the rest of the game) [/spoiler] [spoiler] The Oracle's home is a crazy maze biome where the areas do not even properly connect. It is a massive labyrinth that feels quite rewards once you figure out how to navigate. There are some tricky puzzles here [/spoiler] [spoiler] End of the world (dragon biome) features cool looking dragon enemies but is really a crossroads between the others [/spoiler] [spoiler] Abyss biome is the most generic of the late game areas but offers good insight to the story [/spoiler]  And my favorite... [spoiler] THE SPACE BIOME which features planet hopping and orbital gravity. This for real felt like I was playing super mario galaxy in a metroidvania and the platforming segments get diabolical here. I loved this area for how unique it felt in the 2d-sidescroller space and the idea of your character literally planet hopping with his super abilities is badass  [/spoiler]  This does not even cover all of them and there are some other cool secrets to explore including the end game area and multiple secret boss areas.  I really liked the quest system in this game as it keeps track of all your ongoing missions with some brief information to remind you of what to do. I think there could be more information here in the form of hints as some of these were very confusing. What's cool to me is how interconnected the quests feel as you will get a quest in one part of the world that requires you to do things in later areas that you might not have even unlocked yet.  The combat isn't bad, but it felt too simple for me. Mobs are whatever and you can just face-tank them and then kill them with your invulnerability frames. The bosses are different though as you must learn their patterns and look for openings. The boss fights are all cool and exciting and I enjoyed battling them. What I like about the boss design is that you can't just dps race them as they will always win out. You really need to learn how to fight them without getting hit. There is one weapon that really trivializes everything: The [spoiler] Katana has incredible range and very high damage. Using this weapon feels like a cheat code over the rest. [/spoiler] I will say that running around in lategame, teleporting around and one-shotting mobs with this does feel pretty great though.  The platforming in this game is AMAZING. You will see many haters in the reviews but in my opinion the platforming in this game is what sets it apart from the rest. There is an optional mode which removes the platforming challenges in the world. If you do not enjoy precision platforming, then I recommend playing in the aeterna mode so that you do not tear your hair out in frustration and then quit and leave a negative review. If you do enjoy precision platforming and suffering, then you are in for a treat. Some of the challenges make the Hollow Knight's path of pain look like just another walk through the park and some will really test your resolve and patience. I see complaints saying that there are not enough rewards for traversing difficult paths, but honestly it feels that the reward is in triumphing through the challenge itself. Sometimes you will "softlock" yourself in the middle of a platforming bit, but I recommend always carrying a return potion for these cases. Also though there is no reality in which you truly lock yourself out as any place you can platform into, you can platform out of.  The map and exploration are interesting and I have some opinions here. I am not a fan of loading screens, and am a much bigger fan of a fully interconnected world (Grime is a good example) but that is not possible with what the devs were trying to achieve with this map. The map itself is very large though and I had a blast exploring all of aeterna. You can buy a treasure map from the map maker which marks all secrets for an area for you so that on a second pass of the area you can make sure you don't miss anything.   All in all, this has become one of my all-time favorite metroidvanias after playing and I CAN'T wait until the sequel comes out.   Thank you to the devs for curating an incredible and unique experience that is targeted towards players like me and not folding to a barrage of negative reviews focusing on difficulty.

Рекомендую!

Aeterna Noctis is a game that is too irritating to really suggest for other people to play.  The game itself has a number of bugs and issues that, while not game breaking, are very annoying.  Any time you die, you will see the room you're in reload, then you will see a black screen and respawn.  During some cutscenes, you'll retain control of the character and jump as you move through the dialogue. Sometimes hits wont register, or you'll dodge in a direction you don't want to, and various other minor, but extremely annoying bugs.  The game itself, even without bugs, is also far more infuriating than it is fun.  I'm all for difficult games, so long as the difficulty feels good, but this game really pushes it too far.  For reference, I played about half the game on hard mode, then got fed up and moved to normal.  Normal mode makes platforming challenges about right, but it doesn't really help with bosses.  Enemies and bosses generally have about double the health in hard mode, but nothing else about them changes.  This wouldn't be so bad if not for the fact that even in normal mode enemies and bosses have about twice as much health as they aught to.  The difficulty of the game really comes from attacks that often come without warning, or the proper ability to dodge combined with overly long boss fights.  This isn't a terrible game, but it is a game that is tuned for people that desire much more challenge than most people will enjoy.  I've completed all the Fromsoft games, along with Lies of P, Ender Lilies, Blasphemous 1 + 2, etc. I've played and enjoyed many games that are considered difficult, this game really goes too far and becomes tedious rather than fun.  After completing pretty much every boss, rather than feeling triumph, I was just bored.

Aeterna Noctis is a game I found when trying to scratch the Metroidvania itch I had. In short, it's a fantastic game, combat feels great with a variety of ways to build, and exploration is... well top notch, which includes difficulty.   The map of Aeterna Noctis is girthy, with secrets and back-tracking fully utilized. It's not a complaint, but a warning, there are platforming puzzles that are BRUTAL in this game. Like sit there for a few hours as you memorize each section brutal. Patience will win out, but you have to be diligent. And sometimes these puzzles are not well placed.  This is mainly a critic for the devs. In Hollow Knight, the hardest jumping puzzles, in the Grey Castle, really just showed cool lore, a trinket for the "true" ending, and weren't holding in game power. Aeterna Noctis has these BRUTAL puzzles which lock actual power behind them and that can really feel frustrating as it feels like you can't progress the game without passing said BRUTAL puzzles. I certainly feel pride in beating those puzzles, but I won't be installing the game... any time soon because I don't want to go through those puzzles again lol.  If you're looking for a Metroidvania game with some real GIRTH to it, I cannot recommend Aeterna Noctis enough. But don't go crying to the internet if you can't take more than 5 minutes to solve a puzzle.

What a massive and awesome Metroidvania!  53 hours (in-game time; difficulty: noctis) for 97.5% of the game and only 33% of steam achievements. This is a lot of bang for the buck.  Caution: This game is hard. If you like it hard and sometime frustrating, buy this game. There are a lot of check points so you can try a lot of passages over and over again (and often you have to). And (!) there is a lot of platforming. I myself wouldn't consider myself a friend of excessive platforming in Metroidvania, however in this game I fell in love with.

My quest for the great metroidvania experience hit yet another bump with Aeterna Noctis. It seems that no matter how many things devs do right, they inevitably sc** a few things up beyond belief. Aeterna Noctis has a great story, a great atmosphere and surprisingly good platforming mechanics, and even the checkpointing system is reasonable. The great atmosphere turns unbearably depressing after 15-20 minutes - switching off the music helps. The same great atmosphere is supported by great visuals, which are rather poorly implemented technically - the amount of loading the game does between the usually small scenes is shocking, and whenever there are serious particle effects on the screen there are huge drops in the framerate. At the same time, the awesomely multilayered goblin caverns run without a hiccup... The map shenanigans are hardly even worth mentioning - the game has a very capable automapping feature, and true to the genre, it drops many come-back-later locations. It even provides mapping pins to mark these - but you have to buy these gradually, and any given moment I'd need around 3x as much as available. Why???? What really did me in in this game were the bosses. Not the difficulty, although the sword boss fight felt interminable - it was the audiovisuals, which is something new. There's the graveyard boss, that goes bwoaaaaaa like every 3 seconds - after 3-4 minutes it was incredibly painful. And then the phoenix boss, which looks absolutely amazing, but is so heavy on the bright colours that I had to quit after 15 mins of trying, and I couldn't see properly for some further 10-15 mins afterwards. I wonder how this stuff got through testing... (sigh)  Update: I found a way to get through the phoenix without getting my eyes burned out. For a while, the game was excellent, then starting with the Dream Kingdom, it went downhill again. Some of the locations are beautiful, but they are oh-so-empty, and some quests seem to be deliberately aimed at wasting the players' time. Prime example is the Celestial Dragon, but there are also others. There's a lot of fun to be had with the game, and there's a lot of frustration, not due to difficulty. Still no thumbs up.

簡単な方でクリア。 Noctis（トゲトラップ満載の方）は途中で詰まって断念しようか考え中です。ちなみに実績はNoctisにもたくさんあります。  最初は、めんどくさいトラップ、前景に隠れて見えにくい画面、分かりにくいインベントリ画面、説明不足のクエストなど不満な点が多くてつまんなかったです。  でもせっかく買ったしと思ってYouTubeを見ながら少しずつ進めていくと、中盤以降はかなり面白くなり、すっかりハマってしまいました。 ジャンプ→弓撃ち→移動→ダブルジャンプ→空中ダッシュ→さらに弓 みたいなのを一瞬でやらないといけないような高難度アクションが満載です。 天地さかさま操作も入ってきて、えらいことになります。 でも、意外とすぐに慣れて楽しくなってきます。  初見ではとても勝てそうにないボスファイトも、動画見ながらパターンを覚えていくと、だんだんとコツが掴めてくる。 なんならノーダメージ攻略も見えてくる。 そういうのが楽しい方にはぜひおすすめしたいです。

What a horrible drag of a game. Its length is way too big for the obnoxious and clunky traversal mechanics, and the insufferable combat (no parrying, no i-frames while dashing, only some trash ability that protects you for 0.1 seconds that you can use once every 5-10 seconds).  While it is a classic trope in the metroidvania genre to start with few traversal abilities and acquire more over the progress of the game, here it feels horrendous to do anything while missing these abilities. Adding to this, the overwhelming amount of places where you cannot go because of missing abilities makes this game feel like a Backtracking Simulator - you are going to enter several areas where there are like 6-7 places where you could go, but only 1 of which being available with your current start abilities. To add to this nightmare, a lot of places are blocked by simply some "purple barrier" that you cannot pass until you have the last ability which is unlocked after like 15+ hours in the game, which basically means that you have 2 choices: backtrack to all other locations after getting the second-to-last ability, leaving you with the sour taste of having to BACKTRACK YET AGAIN in the future, or just straight up skip going back - which leads to you missing power-ups of your health, or other useful stuff that you may find.  On the topic of "useful stuff that you find", this game feels so unrewarding. 70% of the things you find are chests filled with gold, which you can spend mainly in one location, or at a map vendor which appears in each area of the game. Chests usually contain somewhere between 800 and 3k-4k gold, and if you are a completionist and don't mind backtracking for tens of hours, the map vendor has the generous offer of giving you all places where the collectibles are, at exaggerated prices of 30k gold plus (while you would get from those chests around 10k if you're lucky).  One more big complaint that I have is that, after you acquire a traversal ability, the devs think that you become a master at using it immediately, and start sprinkling in horribly annoying platforming sections.  What would have made this game bearable (and, why not, a nice try at a hard platforming metroidvania game) was if the size of all areas were halved and they cut down on the useless backtracking stuff, because coherency in this game is like a needle in a haystack.

As a fan of metroidvanias, I really wanted to like this game, but it makes egregiously bad design decisions at every turn.   Boss fights were the final nail in the coffin that made me drop it; the player hitbox is huge, but the boss hitboxes feel inconsistent and inaccurate.  You'll feel like you found the rhythm and form of a fight only to find yourself swinging at air.  I've tried to pick this one up twice, falling off around the same point both times, and likely won't be picking it up again.

Есть кристалл, увеличивающий время и еще один для самонаведения на цель. И т.д. Оперируя кристаллами и древом навыков  можно расковырять любого босса на раз-два. При наличии прямых рук и нормальной реакции, разумеется. Игра требует, чтобы в нее играли хорошо, иначе никакого удовольствия она не доставит. Придется учиться в нее играть.
